title: Polynomial Bounds for Birch's Theorem on Forms
speaker: Amichai Lampert
abstract: Consider a collection of forms of odd degree with rational coefficients. Birch proved in 1957 that if the number of variables is sufficiently large, then the forms must have a nontrivial rational zero. The bounds resulting from Birch's proof, however, are so large that he has described them as "not even astronomical". We prove that, for any fixed odd degree, the number of variables may be taken polynomial in the number of equations. This was previously known only in degree three, by a result of Schmidt from 1982. We will review Birch's original argument, discuss a stronger result by Schmidt and sketch the proof of our theorem. Joint work with Andrew Snowden and Tamar Ziegler.

proof_stages.label: Many-variable odd-degree forms and the polynomial-bound goal
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames the problem of rational zeros of homogeneous odd-degree systems, recalls Baker, Peck, Birch, and previous non-explicit/tower/Ackermann-type bounds, and states the new result that Birch's variable threshold can be polynomial in the number of forms for fixed odd degree.

proof_stages.label: Classical Birch diagonalization and lower-degree proliferation
proof_stages.summary: Birch's original proof is sketched by expanding an odd-degree form on sums of vectors, inductively killing mixed terms to find rational subspaces where the form becomes diagonal, and then applying Peck's diagonal theorem, while noting that one degree-d equation produces many lower-degree equations.

proof_stages.label: Schmidt strength and absolute regularization
proof_stages.summary: Strength is defined as the minimal number of products of lower-degree forms needed to express a form, extended to pencils of forms, and Schmidt's high-strength circle-method theorem gives Zariski-dense rational points and a regularization proof of Birch that remains inefficient.

proof_stages.label: Relative strength and efficient relative regularization
proof_stages.summary: Relative strength is introduced as strength modulo lower-degree equations, and a theorem for quintics relative to cubics gives Zariski-dense rational zeroes with the quintic threshold independent of the number of cubic equations, enabling an efficient regularization process and polynomial bounds.

proof_stages.label: Restriction-map surjectivity from relative strength
proof_stages.summary: The main technical input asserts that, under the relative-strength hypotheses, the map sending an m-tuple of ambient vectors to the restrictions of the cubic and quintic systems on their span is surjective for m on the order of r squared, with Q-to-C strength loss controlled via multilinearization and auxiliary geometric/finite-field arguments.

proof_stages.label: Schmidt applied on a rational subspace
proof_stages.summary: Using surjectivity, one chooses a rational subspace inside the cubic zero locus on which the restricted quintics are generic and hence high-strength, then applies Schmidt first to produce the rational subspace satisfying the cubics and again to solve the restricted quintic system.

examples.name: Pythagorean triples
examples.note: Pythagorean triples are mentioned as a familiar low-variable homogeneous equation with infinitely many rational solutions, contrasting with later many-variable existence theorems.
examples.engagement_reason: Pythagorean triples are only cited as an old example with infinitely many solutions, with no computation or analysis.

examples.name: Selmer's locally soluble cubic with no rational point
examples.note: Selmer's example illustrates that in few variables a homogeneous equation can have no rational solution for genuinely global reasons despite no local obstruction.
examples.engagement_reason: Selmer’s example is invoked for flavor as locally soluble but globally insoluble, without working through the cubic or its obstruction.

passes.reason: Selmer’s cubic is used as a Hasse-principle failure: locally soluble everywhere but with no rational point.
Selmer’s smooth plane cubic is used as a Hasse-principle failure: locally soluble but with no rational point.

examples.name: Baker's seven-variable diagonal cubic theorem
examples.note: The theorem that every seven-variable diagonal cubic of the displayed type has a rational zero, with seven variables tight, is used as a concrete instance of the many-variable odd-degree phenomenon.
examples.engagement_reason: Baker’s seven-variable diagonal cubic theorem is stated and contextualized as a cited result, but not applied or derived on the example.

passes.reason: Baker’s seven-variable diagonal cubic result is presented at the tight minimal variable threshold, since six variables may fail.

examples.name: Norm-form local obstruction construction
examples.note: An audience member describes norm-form equations arranged modulo a prime to produce local obstructions, illustrating possible lower-bound phenomena for the number of variables.
examples.engagement_reason: The norm-form local obstruction is only sketched as a kind of lower-bound construction, with no concrete verification of the obstruction.
